text: The surgical setup includes four ports: two 10 mm ports for the camera and instruments, one 12 mm port for potential stapler use, and a 5 mm port for liver retraction. The video displays the specific positions of these ports on the patient's abdomen.
The camera port is positioned in the paramedian area, four fingerbreadths lateral to the oblique muscles. Working ports are placed on either side, and a sublipoid port is used for liver retraction.

The surgeon begins dissection by detaching the liver attachments to expose the kidney and adrenal gland in the supracolic compartment. After freeing the attachments, the liver is retracted using the subcephoid port, revealing the adrenal tumor.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to divide the peripheral attachments of the adrenal gland, minimizing handling due to the presence of pheochromocytoma to prevent blood pressure surges.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to divide the liver attachments of the adrenal tumor and gland, ensuring no retraction or traction is applied to the adrenal gland or tumor during the procedure.

The surgeon begins medial dissection, excising the duodenum attachments to expose the inferior vena cava (IVC) beneath. Using a hook cautery, the flimsy adhesions between the adrenal gland and the IVC are carefully divided, ensuring no injury to the IVC.
The surgeon applies mild traction to the adrenal gland, coordinating with the anesthetist to manage blood pressure surges. The adrenal vein is now visible and being dissected.

The surgeon carefully dissects the right-sided adrenal vein, which appears shorter and stockier. The vein is then doubly clipped on the patient's side and a single clip is applied on the specimen side before it is divided.

The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects an accessory adrenal vein on the right side, which is then clipped and divided. Adjacent to it, the adrenal artery is also doubly clipped on the patient's side and divided using a ligature on the specimen side.

After dividing the vessel, the adrenal gland is lifted and freed from surrounding attachments. The surgeon continues dissection in the plane, progressively lifting the adrenal gland while applying traction. Fibro-fatty tissues are divided using a ligature device.

The surgeon carefully divides the lower attachments of the perinephric fat using a ligature, ensuring no adrenal tissue is left behind. The dissection progresses from the medial to the lateral aspect, connecting with the previously completed lateral dissection.

After completing the dissection, the surgical field is irrigated with saline to identify any small bleeders or oozing areas, which are then ligated or cauterized using electrocautery and ligature. A drain is placed through the lateral port, and the adrenal specimen is secured in an endo bag.
